<style>
  .math-post{font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:1.05rem;line-height:1.9;color:#2d2d2d;max-width:860px;margin:0 auto}

  .pdf-btn .pi{font-size:1.5rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt{display:flex;flex-direction:column;text-align:left;line-height:1.35}
  .pdf-btn .pt1{font-size:1rem}
  .pdf-btn .pt2{font-size:.72rem;opacity:.82;font-weight:400}

</style>

<div class="math-post">

<!-- §1 HERO + STATS -->
<div class="post-hero">
  <h1>Functions and Relations</h1>
  <p>Relations and functions are the fundamental language in which all of mathematics is written. Every equation, every transformation, every structure in analysis, algebra, and geometry is ultimately a function between sets. This post builds the complete foundation — from ordered pairs and equivalence relations all the way to bijections, composition, and inverses.</p>
  <a class="lang-switch" href="/blog/functions-and-relations-hindi/">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में पढ़ें</a>
</div>

<div class="stats-strip">
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">3</span><span class="stat-lbl">Types of Function</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">3</span><span class="stat-lbl">Equiv. Rel. Props</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">$f^{-1}$</span><span class="stat-lbl">Inverse ↔ Bijection</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">4</span><span class="stat-lbl">Solved Examples</span></div>
  <div class="stat-item"><span class="stat-val">CSIR/GATE/JAM</span><span class="stat-lbl">Exam Relevance</span></div>
</div>

<!-- §2 DEFINITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ede9fe;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Relations and Equivalence Relations</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Relation</div>
  <p>A <strong>relation</strong> from set $A$ to set $B$ is any subset $R \subseteq A \times B$. We write $a\,R\,b$ to mean $(a, b) \in R$. A <strong>relation on</strong> $A$ is a subset of $A \times A$.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Equivalence Relation</div>
  <p>A relation $\sim$ on $A$ is an <strong>equivalence relation</strong> if it satisfies all three of:</p>
  <p><strong>(i) Reflexive:</strong> $a \sim a$ for all $a \in A$.</p>
  <p><strong>(ii) Symmetric:</strong> $a \sim b \Rightarrow b \sim a$.</p>
  <p><strong>(iii) Transitive:</strong> $a \sim b$ and $b \sim c \Rightarrow a \sim c$.</p>
  <p>The <strong>equivalence class</strong> of $a$ is $[a] = \{x \in A : x \sim a\}$. The collection $A/{\sim}$ of all equivalence classes is called the <strong>quotient set</strong> — it is a partition of $A$ into disjoint non-empty subsets.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Function — Formal Definition</div>
  <p>A <strong>function</strong> $f \colon A \to B$ is a relation from $A$ to $B$ such that every element of $A$ is related to <em>exactly one</em> element of $B$.</p>
  <p><strong>Domain:</strong> $A$ &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Codomain:</strong> $B$ &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Range (Image):</strong> $f(A) = \{f(a) : a \in A\} \subseteq B$</p>
  <p>Note: range $\subseteq$ codomain; equality holds if and only if $f$ is surjective.</p>
</div>

📖 <strong>Reference:</strong> Apostol, T.M., <strong>Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 2nd ed., Ch. 1, §1.3–1.5. Also: Rudin, W., <strong>Principles of Mathematical Analysis</strong>, 3rd ed., Ch. 2, §2.1.

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F517; Prerequisites</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation</strong></a> — sets and Cartesian products are the raw material from which relations and functions are built.<br>
  <strong>&#x2190;</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods</strong></a> — the $\forall/\exists$ quantifiers and implication are used in all injectivity and surjectivity proofs.</p>
</div>

<!-- §3 INTUITION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#d1fae5;">&#x1F4A1;</div>
  <h2>Intuition — Injective, Surjective, Bijective</h2>
</div>

<p>Think of a function $f \colon A \to B$ as an assignment of outputs to inputs. The three key properties describe how "good" this assignment is:</p>

<div class="type-grid">
  <div class="type-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <h4 style="color:#2a5298;">&#x1F9F5; Injective (1-1)</h4>
    <p>No two inputs share the same output.<br>$f(a_1)=f(a_2)\Rightarrow a_1=a_2$</p>
    <p style="margin-top:8px;font-size:.8rem;color:#94a3b8;">Like: each student gets a unique seat</p>
  </div>
  <div class="type-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <h4 style="color:#059669;">&#x1F3AF; Surjective (onto)</h4>
    <p>Every output is hit by at least one input.<br>$f(A) = B$</p>
    <p style="margin-top:8px;font-size:.8rem;color:#94a3b8;">Like: every seat is occupied by someone</p>
  </div>
  <div class="type-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <h4 style="color:#7c3aed;">&#x2194;&#xFE0F; Bijective</h4>
    <p>Both injective AND surjective.<br>Perfect one-to-one correspondence.</p>
    <p style="margin-top:8px;font-size:.8rem;color:#94a3b8;">Like: exactly one student per seat, every seat filled</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="info-card purple">
  <div class="info-card-header purple">&#x1F4D0; Composition and Inverse</div>
  <p><strong>Composition:</strong> If $f \colon A \to B$ and $g \colon B \to C$, then $(g \circ f) \colon A \to C$ with $(g \circ f)(a) = g(f(a))$. Note: apply $f$ first, then $g$.</p>
  <p><strong>Inverse:</strong> $f \colon A \to B$ has an inverse $f^{-1} \colon B \to A$ satisfying $f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_A$ and $f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_B$ <strong>if and only if</strong> $f$ is bijective. Here, $\mathrm{id}_A$ is the identity mapping on the set A, i.e., $\mathrm{id}_A(x) = x $\forall x \in A$.</p>
  <p><strong>Composition inverse law:</strong> $(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}$ (order reverses).</p>
  <p><strong>Composition preserves type:</strong> If $f$ and $g$ are both injective (resp. surjective, bijective), then so is $g \circ f$.</p>
</div>

<div class="info-card hindi">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F1EE;&#x1F1F3; हिंदी में संक्षेप</div>
  <p><strong>सम्बन्ध (Relation)</strong> समुच्चय $A\times B$ का कोई उपसमुच्चय है। <strong>तुल्यता सम्बन्ध (Equivalence Relation)</strong> के लिए तीन गुण चाहिए: स्वतुल्यता (Reflexive), सममिति (Symmetric) और संक्रामकता (Transitive)। <strong>फलन (Function)</strong> $f\colon A\to B$ में प्रत्येक $a\in A$ का ठीक एक प्रतिबिम्ब होता है। <strong>एकैकी (Injective):</strong> भिन्न इनपुट के भिन्न आउटपुट। <strong>आच्छादी (Surjective):</strong> प्रत्येक $b\in B$ का पूर्वप्रतिबिम्ब हो। <strong>द्विएकैकी (Bijective):</strong> दोनों — तभी प्रतिलोम फलन $f^{-1}$ अस्तित्व में है।</p>
</div>

<!-- §4 SOLVED EXAMPLES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x270F;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Solved Examples</h2>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex1">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">1</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Very Easy &nbsp;|&nbsp; Classifying a Function</div>
      <div class="example-title">Show $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=2x+1$ is bijective; find $f^{-1}$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Injective:</strong> Suppose $f(x_1)=f(x_2)$, i.e. $2x_1+1=2x_2+1$. Then $x_1=x_2$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Surjective:</strong> Given $y\in\mathbb{R}$, set $x=\dfrac{y-1}{2}\in\mathbb{R}$. Then $f(x)=2\cdot\dfrac{y-1}{2}+1=y$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Conclusion:</strong> $f$ is bijective with $f^{-1}(y)=\dfrac{y-1}{2}$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex2">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">2</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Easy–Medium &nbsp;|&nbsp; Equivalence Relation</div>
      <div class="example-title">Verify $a \sim b \Leftrightarrow 3\mid(a-b)$ on $\mathbb{Z}$ is an equivalence relation; describe classes</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Reflexive:</strong> $3\mid(a-a)=0$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Symmetric:</strong> If $3\mid(a-b)$, say $a-b=3k$, then $b-a=-3k=3(-k)$, so $3\mid(b-a)$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Transitive:</strong> If $3\mid(a-b)$ and $3\mid(b-c)$, say $a-b=3k$ and $b-c=3m$, then $a-c=3(k+m)$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Equivalence classes (three, partitioning $\mathbb{Z}$):</strong><br>
    $[0]=\{\ldots,-3,0,3,6,\ldots\}$,&nbsp;&nbsp;
    $[1]=\{\ldots,-2,1,4,7,\ldots\}$,&nbsp;&nbsp;
    $[2]=\{\ldots,-1,2,5,8,\ldots\}$. $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex3">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">3</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Medium–Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; Composition and Inverse</div>
      <div class="example-title">Find $(g\circ f)^{-1}$ for $f(x)=x+1$, $g(x)=e^x$; verify $(g\circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1}\circ g^{-1}$</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p>$f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x+1$; $\;g\colon\mathbb{R}\to(0,\infty)$, $g(x)=e^x$.</p>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Composition:</strong> $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))=g(x+1)=e^{x+1}$, so $g\circ f\colon\mathbb{R}\to(0,\infty)$.</p>
    <p><strong>Injective:</strong> $e^{x_1+1}=e^{x_2+1}\Rightarrow x_1=x_2$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Surjective:</strong> For $y>0$, set $x=\ln y-1$; then $(g\circ f)(x)=e^{\ln y}=y$. $\checkmark$</p>
    <p><strong>Inverse:</strong> $(g\circ f)^{-1}(y)=\ln y - 1$.</p>
    <p><strong>Verification:</strong> $f^{-1}(y)=y-1$ and $g^{-1}(y)=\ln y$, so $(f^{-1}\circ g^{-1})(y)=f^{-1}(\ln y)=\ln y-1$. $\checkmark$ $\blacksquare$</p>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="example-card ex4">
  <div class="example-header">
    <div class="example-num">4</div>
    <div>
      <div class="example-label">Hard &nbsp;|&nbsp; CSIR NET / GATE / IIT JAM Level</div>
      <div class="example-title">Prove: $g\circ f$ injective $\Rightarrow f$ injective; $g\circ f$ surjective $\Rightarrow g$ surjective</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <p>Let $f\colon A\to B$ and $g\colon B\to C$.</p>
  <div class="solution-block">
    <p><strong>Part (i): $g\circ f$ injective $\Rightarrow f$ injective.</strong></p>
    <p>Suppose $f(a_1)=f(a_2)$. Then $g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))$, i.e. $(g\circ f)(a_1)=(g\circ f)(a_2)$. Since $g\circ f$ is injective, $a_1=a_2$. Hence $f$ is injective. $\blacksquare$</p>
    <p><strong>Part (ii): $g\circ f$ surjective $\Rightarrow g$ surjective.</strong></p>
    <p>Let $c\in C$. Since $g\circ f$ is surjective, $\exists\,a\in A$ with $(g\circ f)(a)=c$, i.e. $g(f(a))=c$. Setting $b=f(a)\in B$, we have $g(b)=c$. Hence $g$ is surjective. $\blacksquare$</p>
    <p><strong>Remark — converses are FALSE:</strong> $g\circ f$ injective does NOT imply $g$ injective. $g\circ f$ surjective does NOT imply $f$ surjective. (Counter-example: $A=\{1\}$, $B=\{1,2\}$, $C=\{1\}$, $f(1)=1$, $g(1)=g(2)=1$; then $g\circ f$ is surjective but $f$ is not.)</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §5 QUICK REVISION -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#dbeafe;">&#x26A1;</div>
  <h2>Quick Revision Cards</h2>
</div>

<div class="revision-grid">
  <div class="rev-card a">
    <h4>&#x1F4CA; A — Key Definitions</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Injective: $f(a_1)=f(a_2)\Rightarrow a_1=a_2$</li>
      <li>Surjective: $f(A)=B$</li>
      <li>Bijective: injective AND surjective</li>
      <li>$(g\circ f)(a)=g(f(a))$</li>
      <li>$(g\circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1}\circ g^{-1}$</li>
      <li>Equiv. rel.: Reflexive + Symmetric + Transitive</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card b">
    <h4>&#x2699;&#xFE0F; B — Conditions &amp; Edge Cases</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>Range ⊆ Codomain; equal iff surjective</li>
      <li>$f^{-1}$ (function) needs bijectivity</li>
      <li>$f^{-1}(S)$ (preimage of set) always defined</li>
      <li>$g\circ f$ injective $\Rightarrow f$ injective (not $g$)</li>
      <li>$g\circ f$ surjective $\Rightarrow g$ surjective (not $f$)</li>
      <li>Equiv. classes: identical or disjoint</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
  <div class="rev-card c">
    <h4>&#x1F3AF; C — Exam Tips</h4>
    <ul>
      <li>&#x1F535; <strong>CSIR NET:</strong> Disprove injective: find $a_1\neq a_2$ with $f(a_1)=f(a_2)$</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E2; <strong>GATE:</strong> Know both parts of Example 4 + both counterexamples</li>
      <li>&#x1F7E0; <strong>IIT JAM:</strong> Range ≠ Codomain — classic MCQ trap</li>
      <li>&#x1F534; <strong>B.Sc. Raj.:</strong> Verify ALL 3 equiv. rel. properties explicitly</li>
    </ul>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §6 COMMON MISTAKES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fee2e2;">&#x26A0;&#xFE0F;</div>
  <h2>Common Mistakes</h2>
</div>

<div class="warning-box">
  <h4>&#x274C; Errors to Avoid</h4>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 1: Confusing range with codomain</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> "The range of $f(x)=x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is $\mathbb{R}$." &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> Range $=[0,\infty)\subsetneq\mathbb{R}$. Codomain is $\mathbb{R}$; they are equal only when $f$ is surjective.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 2: Assuming every function has an inverse function</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Writing $f^{-1}$ for a non-bijective $f$. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> The inverse <em>function</em> $f^{-1}$ exists if and only if $f$ is bijective. The preimage $f^{-1}(S)$ is always a set, but not always a function.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 3: Reversing the order in composition inverse</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> $(g\circ f)^{-1}=g^{-1}\circ f^{-1}$. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> $(g\circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1}\circ g^{-1}$. The order reverses — like putting on shoes then socks, and reversing: socks off first, then shoes.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="warning-item">
    <div class="warning-label">Error 4: Partial verification of equivalence relations</div>
    <p><strong>Wrong:</strong> Checking only reflexivity and symmetry. &nbsp;|&nbsp; <strong>Correct:</strong> All three properties must be verified. Without transitivity, the relation is merely <em>symmetric</em> — not an equivalence relation.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §7 APPLICATIONS -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#ecfdf5;">&#x1F310;</div>
  <h2>Real-World Applications</h2>
</div>

<div class="app-grid">
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#2a5298;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F512;</div>
    <h4>Cryptographic Hash Functions</h4>
    <p>A hash $h\colon\{0,1\}^*\to\{0,1\}^{256}$ is designed to be computationally injective (collision-resistant) but deliberately NOT surjective or invertible.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#059669;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F5C4;&#xFE0F;</div>
    <h4>Database: Functional Dependencies</h4>
    <p>A functional dependency $A\to B$ in a schema is exactly the statement that the projection onto $A$ is a function — each $A$-value determines a unique $B$-value.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#7c3aed;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4BB;</div>
    <h4>Type Theory (CS)</h4>
    <p>Functions between types are morphisms in the category of types. Bijections are type isomorphisms — lossless, reversible data conversions.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="app-card" style="border-top-color:#d97706;">
    <div class="app-icon">&#x1F4F1;</div>
    <h4>Modular Arithmetic</h4>
    <p>$\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}$ is the quotient set of $\mathbb{Z}$ under congruence mod $n$ — the foundation of clock arithmetic, check digits, and public-key cryptography.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- §8 SUMMARY TABLE -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#e0e7ff;">&#x1F4CB;</div>
  <h2>Summary Table &amp; Key Result</h2>
</div>

<table class="summary-table">
  <thead>
    <tr><th>Concept</th><th>Statement / Formula</th><th>Condition</th><th>Reference</th></tr>
  </thead>
  <tbody>
    <tr><td><strong>Equiv. Relation</strong></td><td>Reflexive + Symmetric + Transitive</td><td>On $A\times A$</td><td>Apostol §1.3</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Injective</strong></td><td>$f(a_1)=f(a_2)\Rightarrow a_1=a_2$</td><td>$f\colon A\to B$</td><td>Apostol §1.4</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Surjective</strong></td><td>$f(A)=B$</td><td>$f\colon A\to B$</td><td>Apostol §1.4</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Bijective</strong></td><td>Injective AND Surjective</td><td>$f\colon A\to B$</td><td>Apostol §1.4</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Inverse exists</strong></td><td>$f^{-1}\colon B\to A$ iff $f$ bijective</td><td>$f\colon A\to B$</td><td>Rudin §2.1</td></tr>
    <tr><td><strong>Comp. inverse</strong></td><td>$(g\circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1}\circ g^{-1}$</td><td>$f,g$ bijective</td><td>Standard</td></tr>
  </tbody>
</table>

<div class="highlight-result">
  <p><strong>The Central Equivalence:</strong></p>
  <p>$$f\colon A\to B \text{ bijective} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; f^{-1}\colon B\to A \text{ exists} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \lvert A\rvert = \lvert B\rvert \text{ (finite sets)}$$</p>
  <p>Every equivalence relation on $A$ partitions $A$ into <strong>disjoint equivalence classes</strong>.</p>
</div>

<!-- §9 CROSS-REFERENCES -->
<div class="section-title">
  <div class="sec-icon" style="background:#fef3c7;">&#x1F517;</div>
  <h2>Cross-References &amp; Related Posts</h2>
</div>

<div class="info-card gold">
  <div class="info-card-header gold">&#x1F4DA; Prerequisites &amp; Next Steps</div>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Prerequisite:</strong> <a href="/blog/sets-and-basic-notation-english/"><strong>Sets and Basic Notation — A Foundation for Mathematics</strong></a> — sets and Cartesian products are the raw material for relations and functions.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2190; Prerequisite:</strong> <a href="/blog/logic-and-proof-methods-english/"><strong>Logic and Proof Methods — The Language of Mathematics</strong></a> — the $\forall/\exists$ quantifiers and implication used in all injectivity and surjectivity proofs.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x2192; Next Topic:</strong> <em>Countability and Cardinality</em> — bijections between infinite sets are the tool for comparing cardinalities of $\mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Q}$, and $\mathbb{R}$.</p>
  <p><strong>&#x1F4D6; Further Reading:</strong> Apostol, Ch. 1, §§1.3–1.6; Rudin, Ch. 2, §§2.1–2.5; Bartle &amp; Sherbert, Ch. 1.</p>
</div>
